content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: This reverse clash is really well done! The concept is clear, and you can figure out what to do fairly quickly. While the solution isn’t extremely difficult, writing it so it works correctly does take some time but overall, it’s a fun and engaging clash, and I love seeing more challenges like this. Great job!

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Despite this being a challenging clash, it was definitely a fun one! The concept is nice, the pattern recognition was instant for me, and clash of code needs more string array clashes anyway. Nice job!
One more thing: are you going to add test cases with the easter egg at the corner of the grid because you don't have those yet?

Cases
* Please add a pair of cases where the difference is located on the rightmost column (but not at any corner) of the grid.

Thanks.

Done! Thank you :)

Also did you know they added buttons for test case reordering :o
Damn, I meant for this to be a beginner-friendly clash :/ Will wait to see what others think! But if it's too difficult I might scrap this clash. Thanks for the feedback @VinceMC.
I have one such case in Test/Validator 8 :)
oh shoot I didn't see that my bad

@VinceMC, which part did you find most challenging?

I'm having a bit of trouble seeing why this clash wouldn't be beginner-friendly. In fact, it might even be too simple to convert into an Easy puzzle. In that case, the task could be adjusted to better match the intended difficulty; for example, by making the arrow size variable instead of fixed, or increasing the number of difference spots.

This is a good idea for a clash. If we want to make it slightly easier, one option would be to remove the cases where the arrow isn't fully visible in the expected output. That'll make out-of-bounds checks unnecessary, and the required logic will become simplified.

Also, regarding implementation: adding explicit out-of-bounds checks is probably the most straightforward approach. A cleaner but more outside-the-box alternative is to add a margin of 2 cells on each side of the grid. That way, we don't have to worry about boundary checks during processing. The only caveat is that we'd need to be careful to exclude those margins correctly when producing the final output.

I'm just new to arrays since I started CoC a month ago. Those who are more experienced shouldn't have to worry.

It truly does depend on if the majority of the CoC community is a beginner or not. If most Clashers are medium-level programmers, then this is definitely a great puzzle as string arrays are very rare nowadays.

(sorry if my explanation doesn't make sense because I am writing this message during class and I can't fully focus)

No worries, your explanation makes perfect sense, and thanks for sharing your perspective! 🙂

As I mentioned in my previous message, I'm happy with whichever approach the author prefers (if they decide to make changes): either simplifying this clash to make it suitable as a beginner challenge, or turning it into a slightly more complex Easy puzzle intended for solving without the pressure of a time limit or competition with others.

Modifying the clash to have the arrows fully visible within the grid loses some of the edge cases I hope to catch clashers with (e.g., with the different character appearing at the top-left of the grid). I'll think about it, but I'll most likely stick with what I have currently unless the feedback is overwhelmingly "too hard" :D
Yes, CG announced it on their Discord server two days ago.
